## William Arthur Waldegrave, Baron Waldegrave of North Hill, PC (born 15 August 1946)

**William Waldegrave** is a British Conservative Party politician who served as a Cabinet minister from 1990 to 1997 and has been a life peer in the House of Lords since 1999. He also served as Provost of Eton College from 2009 to 2024 and was Chancellor of the University of Reading from 2016 to 2022.

### Early Life and Family
Born on 15 August 1946 in London, Waldegrave bore the courtesy title "The Honourable" from birth as the youngest of seven children of **Geoffrey Waldegrave, 12th Earl Waldegrave** and his wife **Mary Hermione Grenfell**. His elder brother is the current 13th Earl Waldegrave. The family's earldom was created five generations earlier for the diplomat and ambassador James Waldegrave, 1st Earl Waldegrave, whose grandfather was King James II.

His sister is **Lady Susan Hussey**, who served as a Woman of the Bedchamber to Queen Elizabeth II and later as a Lady of the Household under King Charles III. He is also the nephew of the courtier Dame Frances Campbell-Preston.

### Education
Waldegrave was educated at **Eton College**, where he won the prestigious Newcastle Scholarship in 1965. He then studied at **Corpus Christi College, Oxford**, where he served as president of both the Oxford Union and the Oxford University Conservative Association. He subsequently attended **Harvard University** on a Kennedy Scholarship. In 1971, he was elected a Prize Fellow of **All Souls College, Oxford**, and was later appointed a Distinguished Fellow in 2001 for his contributions to politics and international relations.

### Early Career
In 1971, Waldegrave joined the **Conservative Research Department** and was then appointed to the **Central Policy Review Staff (CPRS)**, the government's "Think-Tank." He was one of the most active members and was used by Victor Rothschild, head of the CPRS, as a link with the Conservative Party and the outside world. He left in December 1973.

### Parliamentary Career
Waldegrave was elected as **Member of Parliament for Bristol West** in the 1979 general election. He was regarded as a member of the "wet" (moderate) tendency of the Conservative Party, but nonetheless progressed well through ministerial ranks under Margaret Thatcher.

**Junior Ministerial Roles (1981â1990):**
- **Parliamentary Under-Secretary for Education and Science** (1981â1983)
- **Parliamentary Under-Secretary for the Environment** (1983â1985)
- **Minister of State for Local Government** (1985â1986)
- **Minister of State for Environment, Countryside and Planning** (1986â1987)
- **Minister of State for Housing and Planning** (1987â1988)
- **Minister of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs** (1988â1990)

In his Foreign Office role, he was involved in policy on arms exports to Iraq. The Scott Report found he had sent 38 untrue letters to MPs stating the policy was unchanged, though Sir Richard Scott exonerated him of "duplicitous intent."

**Cabinet Positions (1990â1997):**
- **Secretary of State for Health** (1990â1992) â appointed just days before Thatcher's resignation
- **Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster** (1992â1994) â with responsibility for public services and science
- **Minister of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food** (1994â1995)
- **Chief Secretary to the Treasury** (1995â1997)

He remained in the Cabinet throughout John Major's premiership.

### House of Lords
After losing his Bristol West seat to Valerie Davey in the 1997 general election, Waldegrave was created a **life peer** on 28 July 1999 as **Baron Waldegrave of North Hill**, of Chewton Mendip in the County of Somerset.

### Other Notable Activities
- **Rhodes Trust**: Served as trustee (1992â2011) and chair (2002â2011), helping to create the Mandela Rhodes Foundation.
- **National Museum of Science and Industry**: Chairman of trustees (2002â2010).
- **Higgs Boson Prize (1993)**: As British science minister, he famously offered a prize for the best lay explanation of the Higgs boson, leading to David Miller's celebrated metaphor involving Conservative party workers.
- **Private sector**: Director of Coutts & Company and non-executive director of GW Pharmaceuticals.
- **Bilderberg Group**: Attended meetings in 1987, 1988, 1990, and 1995.

### Personal Life
